title: ZenMate VPN Review 2026: Discontinued — Here's What Happened and What to Use Instead
metaDescription: ZenMate VPN was discontinued in May 2023 and merged into CyberGhost VPN. Read our full review covering what happened, where subscribers stand, and the best alternatives.

reviewContent:
## Our Verdict

**ZenMate VPN has been discontinued.** As of May 1, 2023, ZenMate's standalone VPN apps were permanently shut down, and all existing subscribers were migrated to CyberGhost VPN. You cannot purchase a new ZenMate VPN subscription in 2026. The ZenMate website still exists and even displays pricing pages, but clicking through redirects you to CyberGhost. The free browser extensions for Chrome, Firefox, and Edge technically still work, but with severe limitations that make them impractical for any real use.

Before its shutdown, ZenMate earned a **2.0 out of 5** in our assessment. While it offered decent speeds and surprisingly good streaming support at budget prices, critical security shortcomings — including DNS leaks, zero independent audits, and a vague privacy policy from a company based in a 14 Eyes country — made it impossible to recommend even when it was operational. Its discontinuation simply confirms what privacy researchers had been saying for years: ZenMate was a second-tier product that could not compete on its own merits.

If you are a former ZenMate subscriber, the good news is that CyberGhost is a meaningfully better VPN in almost every category. If you landed on this page looking for a budget VPN, skip ZenMate entirely and look at Surfshark or NordVPN instead.

## The Full Story: What Happened to ZenMate?

ZenMate was founded in 2013 in Berlin, Germany, originally as a simple browser extension for Chrome. The founding team — Simon Specka and Markus Hänel — built it as an easy-to-use privacy tool aimed at everyday internet users, not security professionals. The browser extension gained significant traction, eventually reaching over 45 million users worldwide.

In 2018, ZenMate was acquired by **Kape Technologies** (formerly Crossrider), the same company that owns CyberGhost VPN, Private Internet Access (PIA), and ExpressVPN. This acquisition was the beginning of the end for ZenMate as an independent product.

After the acquisition, ZenMate continued to operate as a separate brand, but development gradually slowed. The apps received fewer updates, customer support quality declined, and the server network stagnated while competitors expanded aggressively. By late 2022, it was clear that Kape was channeling resources into its flagship products — CyberGhost and ExpressVPN — rather than maintaining ZenMate.

On **March 16, 2023**, ZenMate officially announced it was "joining forces with CyberGhost VPN." The announcement framed the merger as a benefit for subscribers, giving them access to CyberGhost's larger server network and more advanced features. On **May 1, 2023**, ZenMate's standalone VPN apps were permanently deactivated. Existing subscribers could log into CyberGhost using their ZenMate credentials for the remainder of their subscription period.

The zenmate.com website remains online in 2026, still displaying pricing pages and feature lists. However, the homepage now prominently displays a banner reading "We're joining forces with CyberGhost VPN!" and all purchase links redirect to CyberGhost offers. The free browser extensions remain available in the Chrome Web Store and Firefox Add-ons, though they are extremely limited.

## Who Was ZenMate For?

Before its discontinuation, ZenMate positioned itself as a VPN for:

- **Budget-conscious users** who wanted unlimited device connections at a low price
- **Casual streamers** who needed to unblock Netflix, Disney+, and other platforms
- **Browser-only users** who wanted a simple Chrome or Firefox extension
- **Beginners** who needed a one-click VPN without complex configuration

## Who Was ZenMate NOT For?

- **Privacy-focused users** — no independent audits, DNS leaks discovered, 14 Eyes jurisdiction
- **Power users** — limited protocol options, no advanced configuration
- **Linux users** — no dedicated Linux app was available
- **Anyone who needed reliable support** — customer service collapsed before the shutdown
- **Users in restrictive countries** — no obfuscation or stealth features for China, Iran, etc.

## Historical Speed Test Results

Since ZenMate is no longer operational, we cannot conduct current speed tests. The following results are compiled from historical testing by Cloudwards, vpnMentor, and Comparitech before the May 2023 shutdown.

### Nearby Server Performance (Historical)

On a baseline connection of approximately 165 Mbps, ZenMate delivered the following results:

- **Cambodia (nearest server)**: 146 Mbps download — 11% speed loss
- **Belgium**: 118 Mbps download — 28% speed loss
- **Local servers (vpnMentor test)**: 9% speed loss on average

These nearby-server speeds were respectable and placed ZenMate in the upper-middle tier of VPN providers at the time. For everyday browsing, streaming, and video calls, the performance was more than adequate.

### Long-Distance Performance (Historical)

vpnMentor's testing found a 22% speed loss on nearby servers and 56% on distant servers. Cloudwards recorded the following specific results:

- **Chicago (US)**: 76 Mbps download — 54% speed loss
- **Australia**: 133 Mbps download — 19% speed loss (surprisingly good)

The Australia result is notable — it was unexpectedly fast for such a distant server. However, this may have been due to server load at the time of testing rather than consistent performance.

### Free Extension Performance

The free browser extension was essentially unusable for anything beyond basic text browsing. vpnMentor recorded a **91% speed loss on local servers** and **close to 99% on distant servers** with the free version. This was clearly designed to push users toward the paid Ultimate plan.

| Server Location | Download | Speed Loss | Source |
|----------------|----------|------------|--------|
| Nearest (Cambodia) | 146 Mbps | 11% | Cloudwards |
| Belgium | 118 Mbps | 28% | Cloudwards |
| Chicago, US | 76 Mbps | 54% | Cloudwards |
| Australia | 133 Mbps | 19% | Cloudwards |
| Free extension (local) | ~15 Mbps | 91% | vpnMentor |
| Free extension (distant) | ~2 Mbps | 99% | vpnMentor |

## Security & Privacy

This is where ZenMate fell short — critically so for a product that marketed itself as a privacy tool.

### No Independent Audits

In the entire history of ZenMate VPN, **no independent security audit was ever conducted**. Not a single third-party firm was engaged to verify ZenMate's no-logs claims, test its infrastructure, or evaluate its code. Compare this to NordVPN (six audits by Deloitte and PwC), Mullvad (eight audits by Cure53, Assured AB, and others), or even CyberGhost (Deloitte audit in 2022). ZenMate asked users to trust its privacy claims on faith alone — and that trust was not well-placed.

### DNS Leak Issues

Cloudwards discovered **active DNS leaks** during testing. Even with ZenMate connected and DNS leak protection enabled, traffic was being routed to Google DNS servers in Belgium. This means that despite ZenMate's encryption, your DNS queries — which reveal every website you visit — were potentially visible to Google and your ISP.

DNS leaks are a fundamental security failure for a VPN. They undermine the entire purpose of the product. While ZenMate's app did include a DNS leak protection toggle, the fact that leaks persisted even with protection enabled suggests a deeper architectural problem that was never resolved before the shutdown.

### Encryption

ZenMate used **AES-256-bit encryption** on its desktop and mobile apps, which is the industry standard and perfectly secure. However, the browser extensions only used **AES-128-bit encryption** — weaker, though still practically unbreakable. The inconsistency between app and extension encryption levels was never adequately explained by ZenMate.

### Protocols

Protocol support varied by platform and was poorly documented:

- **Windows**: IKEv2/IPSec and OpenVPN
- **Android**: IKEv2, OpenVPN, and WireGuard (the only platform with WireGuard)
- **macOS/iOS**: IKEv2 only — no OpenVPN or WireGuard

The lack of OpenVPN on macOS and iOS was a significant limitation. IKEv2 is a solid protocol, but having no fallback option created compatibility issues on some networks.

### Privacy Policy and Jurisdiction

ZenMate was headquartered in **Berlin, Germany**. Germany is a member of the **14 Eyes intelligence-sharing alliance** and the European Union. While Germany has relatively strong consumer privacy protections under GDPR, it also has mandatory data retention laws for telecommunications companies.

ZenMate's privacy policy was consistently criticized by reviewers for being **vague and contradictory**. The policy claimed a "strict no-logs policy" but did not clearly define what constituted "logs" or specify exactly what data was or was not collected. Multiple review sites flagged contradictory statements on ZenMate's website about its logging practices.

### Kill Switch

ZenMate did include a kill switch on its Windows desktop app that worked as intended during testing — blocking all internet traffic when the VPN connection dropped. However, the kill switch was not available on all platforms, and its implementation was basic compared to Mullvad's always-on approach or NordVPN's advanced kill switch options.

## Streaming Performance (Historical)

Streaming was actually one of ZenMate's stronger features before the shutdown. vpnMentor reported successful unblocking of multiple platforms:

| Platform | Worked? | Notes |
|----------|---------|-------|
| Netflix US | Yes | Required streaming-optimized servers |
| Disney+ | Yes | Worked on dedicated servers |
| BBC iPlayer | Yes | UK servers unblocked reliably |
| Amazon Prime Video | Yes | Multiple regions supported |
| Hulu | Yes | US servers worked |
| HBO Max | Yes | Streaming servers required |
| Crunchyroll | Yes | Reliable access |

ZenMate offered **streaming-optimized servers** specifically designed to bypass geo-restrictions. These dedicated servers were separate from the standard server list and were regularly updated to maintain access. Standard servers typically could not bypass streaming blocks.

However, all of this is now irrelevant. ZenMate's streaming capabilities ceased to exist when the apps were shut down in May 2023. Former ZenMate subscribers now use CyberGhost, which offers comparable streaming performance with a larger server network.

## Historical Pricing

Before its discontinuation, ZenMate offered some of the lowest prices in the VPN industry:

| Plan | Monthly Cost | Billing Period | Total | Money Back |
|------|-------------|----------------|-------|------------|
| Monthly | $10.99 | 1 month | $10.99 | 30 days |
| 6 Months | $5.39/mo | 6 months | $32.34 | 30 days |
| 18 Months + 3 Free | **$1.99/mo** | 21 months | $41.79 | 30 days |

The $1.99/month price on the long-term plan was genuinely competitive — cheaper than Surfshark and NordVPN at the time. ZenMate also offered a free browser extension, though as noted above, its 2 Mbps speed cap and 4-server limitation made it practically useless.

Payment methods included Visa, Mastercard, American Express, and PayPal. Unlike Mullvad or Proton VPN, ZenMate did not accept cryptocurrency or cash payments.

**None of these plans are available for new subscribers in 2026.** The website's pricing pages still display plans, but all purchase flows redirect to CyberGhost.

## Apps & Platform Support

ZenMate offered apps for a broad range of platforms:

- **Desktop**: Windows, macOS (no Linux)
- **Mobile**: Android, iOS
- **Browser Extensions**: Chrome, Firefox, Edge, Opera
- **Other**: Android TV, Fire TV, OpenVPN configuration for routers
- **Simultaneous connections**: Unlimited on paid plans

The unlimited device policy was a genuine advantage over most competitors. NordVPN limits you to 10 devices, and Mullvad to 5. Only Surfshark matched ZenMate's unlimited connections.

The apps themselves were functional but basic. The interface was clean and straightforward — connect with one click, choose from a list of countries. There was no city-level server selection, no advanced settings panel, and limited customization options. For beginners, this simplicity was a feature. For power users, it was a limitation.

### Server Network

At its peak, ZenMate operated approximately **4,700 servers across 81 countries**. This was a respectable network, though smaller than CyberGhost (9,000+ servers), NordVPN (6,800+ servers), or Surfshark (3,200+ servers).

Notably, **34 of ZenMate's server locations (about 17%) were virtual** — meaning the server was physically located in a different country than advertised. Virtual servers are not inherently bad, but they can impact speeds and raise privacy concerns if the physical server location is in a less privacy-friendly jurisdiction.

P2P torrenting was supported in approximately 70 countries, with 11 countries excluded (including Qatar, Saudi Arabia, and the UAE).

## ZenMate vs. CyberGhost vs. Alternatives

Since ZenMate subscribers were migrated to CyberGhost, and since ZenMate is no longer available for new users, here is how the landscape looks in 2026:

| Feature | ZenMate (Historical) | CyberGhost (Current) | NordVPN | Surfshark |
|---------|---------------------|---------------------|---------|----------|
| Status | Discontinued | Active | Active | Active |
| Servers | 4,700 | 11,500+ | 6,800+ | 3,200+ |
| Countries | 81 | 100+ | 130+ | 100+ |
| Devices | Unlimited | 7 | 10 | Unlimited |
| Monthly Price | Was $1.99/mo | $2.19/mo | $3.39/mo | $2.19/mo |
| Independent Audits | 0 | 1 (Deloitte) | 6 (PwC, Deloitte) | 2 (Cure53, Deloitte) |
| Kill Switch | Yes (Windows) | Yes (all platforms) | Yes (all platforms) | Yes (all platforms) |
| Netflix | Yes (was) | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| No-Logs Verified | No | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| WireGuard | Android only | Yes (all platforms) | NordLynx | Yes |
| Jurisdiction | Germany (14 Eyes) | Romania | Panama | Netherlands (EU) |

## What I Don't Like About ZenMate

Even setting aside the discontinuation, ZenMate had fundamental problems that made it difficult to recommend:

1. **Zero independent audits** — In an industry where NordVPN has been audited six times and even smaller players like Mullvad have undergone eight assessments, ZenMate never submitted to a single independent security review. This is the biggest red flag possible for a VPN provider.

2. **Active DNS leaks** — A VPN that leaks DNS queries is not doing its primary job. The fact that leaks persisted even with leak protection enabled suggests the issue was architectural, not just a settings problem.

3. **Customer support collapse** — Before the shutdown, ZenMate's live chat stopped working entirely, and email support went unanswered for weeks. Users who needed help were essentially abandoned.

4. **Vague privacy policy** — The privacy policy never clearly stated what data was collected. For a product whose entire purpose is privacy, this ambiguity is unacceptable.

5. **14 Eyes jurisdiction with no audits** — Being based in Germany within the 14 Eyes alliance is not automatically disqualifying (Mullvad operates from Sweden, also a 14 Eyes member). But Mullvad compensates with radical transparency, open-source code, and eight independent audits. ZenMate offered none of these counterbalances.

6. **Free version was a bait product** — The free browser extension with 4 servers and 2 Mbps speeds was not a functional VPN — it was a marketing funnel designed to convert free users to paid plans.

## What Should Former ZenMate Users Do?

If you still have an active ZenMate subscription, your account has been migrated to **CyberGhost VPN**. You can log into CyberGhost using your ZenMate credentials and access the full CyberGhost service for the remainder of your subscription period. CyberGhost is a meaningful upgrade from ZenMate in most categories — more servers, more countries, better apps, and an actual independent audit.

If your ZenMate subscription has expired and you are looking for a new VPN, here are our recommendations based on what you valued about ZenMate:

- **Best budget option**: **Surfshark** — from $2.19/month with unlimited devices, just like ZenMate offered
- **Best overall**: **NordVPN** — from $3.39/month with the strongest security audit history in the industry
- **Best for streaming**: **CyberGhost** — 11,500+ servers with dedicated streaming-optimized servers, similar to what ZenMate offered
- **Best for privacy**: **Mullvad** — flat rate, anonymous signup, eight independent audits, open-source apps
- **Best free option**: **Proton VPN Free** — the only reputable free VPN with no speed caps or data limits

## FAQ

**Is ZenMate VPN still available in 2026?**
No. ZenMate VPN was discontinued on May 1, 2023. The standalone VPN apps were permanently shut down, and all subscribers were migrated to CyberGhost VPN. The ZenMate website still exists, but you cannot purchase a new ZenMate VPN subscription. The free browser extensions for Chrome, Firefox, and Edge remain available but are severely limited.

**What happened to my ZenMate subscription?**
All ZenMate subscribers were migrated to CyberGhost VPN. You can log into CyberGhost using your existing ZenMate credentials for the remainder of your subscription period. If you experience issues with the transition, contact CyberGhost support directly, as ZenMate's customer support is no longer operational.

**Why was ZenMate shut down?**
ZenMate was acquired by Kape Technologies in 2018, the same company that owns CyberGhost VPN, ExpressVPN, and Private Internet Access. Rather than maintaining four separate VPN brands, Kape consolidated ZenMate into CyberGhost to reduce operational costs and focus development resources on fewer products.

**Is the ZenMate free browser extension still safe to use?**
The free ZenMate browser extensions for Chrome, Firefox, and Edge are still available and technically functional. However, they only use AES-128-bit encryption (weaker than the industry-standard AES-256), are limited to 4 server locations, and have a 2 Mbps speed cap. More importantly, ZenMate never underwent an independent security audit, and its parent company has effectively abandoned the product. We do not recommend using the free extension for any privacy-sensitive activity.

**Is CyberGhost a good replacement for ZenMate?**
Yes. CyberGhost is a substantial upgrade from ZenMate in most categories. It offers 11,500+ servers across 100+ countries (vs. ZenMate's 4,700 in 81), WireGuard protocol support on all platforms, a Deloitte-audited no-logs policy, and streaming-optimized servers similar to what ZenMate offered. The main limitation compared to ZenMate is that CyberGhost allows 7 simultaneous connections versus ZenMate's unlimited.

**Did ZenMate ever have a security audit?**
No. ZenMate VPN was never subjected to an independent security audit by any third-party firm. This was one of the most significant criticisms against the service throughout its operational life. By contrast, its parent-company sibling CyberGhost completed a Deloitte audit, and competitors like NordVPN and Mullvad have undergone multiple assessments.

**Was ZenMate owned by Kape Technologies?**
Yes. ZenMate was acquired by Kape Technologies (formerly Crossrider) in 2018. Kape is a London-listed company that also owns CyberGhost VPN, ExpressVPN, and Private Internet Access (PIA). The acquisition of ZenMate was part of Kape's strategy to consolidate the VPN market. Kape Technologies has faced scrutiny over its corporate history — before rebranding, Crossrider was known for developing adware and browser extensions with questionable privacy practices.

**What is the best alternative to ZenMate in 2026?**
The best alternative depends on what you valued about ZenMate. For budget pricing with unlimited devices, choose Surfshark ($2.19/month). For the best overall security and speed, choose NordVPN ($3.39/month). For streaming with dedicated servers similar to ZenMate's approach, choose CyberGhost ($2.19/month). For maximum privacy, choose Mullvad (EUR 5/month flat rate, anonymous signup).
